macro: The Fed held its target rate at 3.50%-3.75% at the June 17 meeting, with nine of eighteen FOMC officials now penciling in at least one hike before year-end as core PCE inflation rose to 3.4% in May — nearly double the 2% target. The next meeting is July 28-29, and traders expect another hold while officials watch whether tariff- and war-driven price shocks fade. Geopolitically, the Strait of Hormuz remains in a months-long near-total closure that the IEA's Fatih Birol has called the largest oil supply disruption in the history of the global oil market, though US-Iran peace talks resumed in Doha this week. On the data calendar, June's Producer Price Index isn't due until July 15, leaving corporate earnings (Nike, Constellation Brands, General Mills) as this week's main catalysts.
btc_status: Bitcoin is trading near $59,300, sitting below its roughly $62,300 200-week moving average after a monthly decline of about 18%, while the Crypto Fear & Greed Index reads 15 — Extreme Fear. The key level to watch is $58,115 monthly-low support; a reclaim of the $62,000-$65,600 zone would be needed to flip the technical picture bullish.
markets:

CNXC — Tumbled 22% after missing Q2 earnings and revenue estimates and issuing a weak outlook for Q3 and full-year 2026, with no sign of stabilization yet
energy: WTI crude is trading near $69.80/barrel, up about 0.4% on the day, while Brent sits near $74.28, up roughly 2% in the past 24 hours as markets weigh the resumption of US-Iran peace talks in Doha. The EIA's latest Weekly Petroleum Status Report showed commercial crude inventories fell 6.1 million barrels to 412.1 million barrels in the week ended June 19 — 7% below the five-year average and the third consecutive large weekly draw. The Strait of Hormuz remains in near-total closure since the war began February 28, with Rystad Energy modeling a phased reopening starting mid-July that would restore roughly 85% of lost volumes by October.
vix_and_dollar: The VIX sits at 16.54, down about 4% from the prior session and squarely in 'normal' territory (15-20), signaling traders aren't pricing near-term shock despite the ongoing oil crisis. The Dollar Index (DXY) is at 101.34 and rising for a second straight monthly advance, up more than 2% in June on Fed rate-hike expectations and a weak yen — a stronger dollar that tightens global financial conditions and pressures gold and other USD-denominated commodities.
mortgage_rate: The 30-year fixed mortgage rate averaged 6.49% for the week of June 25, 2026 per Freddie Mac's Primary Mortgage Market Survey, up slightly from 6.47% the week before. Rates have now held in a tight 6.47%-6.53% band for six straight weeks, keeping housing affordability stuck near multi-decade lows with little momentum in either direction.
personal_finance_deep_dive: The University of Michigan's consumer sentiment index cratered to 53.3 in March 2026 — deep in recession territory — even though unemployment has held steady at 4.3%. The disconnect signals households are far more anxious about their financial trajectory than the headline labor market suggests, likely driven by living costs, particularly housing, rising faster than wages. This kind of sentiment collapse without a matching jump in unemployment is unusual and tends to precede pullbacks in discretionary spending as households turn defensive ahead of any actual job losses.
wealth_building_deep_dive: The average 401(k) total savings rate — employee plus employer contributions — hit a record 14.4% in Q1 2026, with the average employee deferral rate also at a record 9.6%, according to Fidelity. Auto-escalation, which automatically bumps a saver's deferral rate each year, is doing much of the work: it drove roughly 31% of participants' contribution increases and contributed to 45% of savers raising their savings rate overall in a shaky economic quarter. The takeaway is structural, not behavioral — if your plan offers auto-escalation, turning it on removes the annual willpower decision entirely; a saver starting at 6% and escalating 1% a year reaches a 14%+ rate in under a decade without ever having to 'decide' to save more.
